<img src="https://r2cdn.perplexity.ai/pplx-full-logo-primary-dark%402x.png" style="height:64px;margin-right:32px"/>

# Designing a Multi-Agent System for Pattern-Driven Trading: Five Implementation Paths

The attached intraday scanner logs reveal that a small subset of tickers consistently dominates the **“biggest winner”** and **“biggest loser”** rankings—e.g., GWAV, EDHL and ELAB repeatedly top the gainer lists while ELBM, LABX and GWH dominate the loser lists.  Cross-checking the five scanners shows seven **multi-metric winners** (appearing in both gain-since-open and percentage-gain feeds plus the high-volume feed) and five **multi-metric losers** (appearing in both loss feeds).  These overlaps provide objective momentum and reversal signals that a multi-agent system can harvest for alpha while controlling crash risk documented in momentum literature.  Below we translate the data findings into a pattern-driven trading framework and lay out **five concrete system-architecture paths**—each suited to different scaling, compliance and research needs.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5][^6][^7]

## Scanner Data Exploration

### Scanner Structure

Each CSV row contains a timestamp followed by **50 ranked symbols** from one of five feeds: *Gain-Since-Open*, *Loss-Since-Open*, *Pct-Gain*, *Pct-Loss* and *Hot-By-Volume*.  A parser can split on commas, strip the “_STK” suffix, and map `(timestamp, rank, symbol)` tuples into a time-series database.[^8][^1][^2][^3][^4]

### Consistent Winners \& Losers

Across the first trading hour GWAV, EDHL, ELAB, AQMS, NVA and SSKN never fall out of the top-10 percentage-gain list, signalling sustained upside momentum.  Conversely ELBM, LABX, GWH, STI and GTI remain fixed in the top-10 percentage-loss list, indicating persistent downside pressure.  Such **persistence** supports both continuation (momentum) and contrarian (oversold bounce) plays depending on context.[^2][^3][^5][^6]

### Cross-Scanner Patterns

A cross-join of the five feeds highlights:

* **Multi-metric winners** → GWAV, EDHL, ELAB, AQMS, NVA, SSKN, JDZG.[^1][^2][^4]
* **Multi-metric losers** → ELBM, LABX, GWH, STI, GTI.[^3]
These stocks form the seed universe for pattern agents, reducing the search space from thousands of tickers to under twenty per interval.[^2][^4][^1][^3]


## Pattern-Based Strategy Framework

| Pattern | Identification Signals | Trading Logic | Risk Control |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Strong Uptrend | Gain-Since-Open ∧ Pct-Gain ∧ High Volume | Momentum continuation long | Trailing stop \& stair-step profit-take |
| Volume Breakout | Hot-By-Volume spike ∧ ≥ 2 σ move | Breakout scalping | 1R fixed stop; exit on volume fade |
| Oversold Bounce | Loss-Since-Open ∧ Pct-Loss ∧ Volume uptick | Contrarian long | Hard stop at intraday low; 2R target |
| Profit-Taking Flag | Pct-Gain top-10 ∧ Volume decays | Fade short | Time-stop if no follow-through |
| Sector Rotation | ≥ 3 gainers from same GICS group | Basket long/short | Sector beta hedge |

Agents specialised for each pattern publish **confidence-weighted signals** to a strategy layer that arbitrates among momentum, contrarian and neutrality options.[^9][^10][^11]

## Five Implementation Paths

### Path 1 – Hierarchical Agent Architecture

A **master-coordinator** routes market data to layered specialist agents—scanner parsers, pattern detectors, strategy deciders, risk controllers and order managers.  Centralised control simplifies auditing but creates a single point of failure and caps horizontal scalability.[^12][^13][^14]

**Best for**: broker-dealer desks needing clear supervisory control and regulatory audit trails.[^12]

### Path 2 – Distributed Pattern-Recognition System

Dozens of **peer agents** independently analyse patterns, then reconcile conflicting views via a Byzantine-fault-tolerant consensus engine before execution.  The design is latency-tolerant and fault-resilient but demands sophisticated orchestration and deadlock mitigation.[^15][^16]

**Best for**: high-frequency prop shops that value fault tolerance over ease of debugging.[^14]

### Path 3 – Reinforcement-Learning Multi-Agent System

Multiple RL agents (e.g., MARL PPO) learn cooperative or competitive behaviours inside a simulated limit-order-book environment, continually adapting policies with experience replay.  This uncovers novel trade sequences but requires vast compute and careful reward shaping to avoid destabilising feedback loops.[^17][^18]

**Best for**: research labs seeking adaptive, self-improving strategies in non-stationary markets.[^12][^18]

### Path 4 – Hybrid Rule-Based + AI System

A deterministic **rule engine** (technical filters, compliance limits) gates an AI layer that employs CNN/LSTM/Transformer models for complex pattern recognition.  A fusion layer weights rule and AI outputs, offering interpretability plus learning capacity.[^19][^7][^20][^21]

**Best for**: institutional managers who must explain trades while exploiting ML edge.[^22]

### Path 5 – Microservices-Based Trading Platform

Each functional slice—data, pattern, strategy, risk, execution—runs as an independent container behind an API gateway, communicating via Kafka streams.  Teams can deploy, scale and version components autonomously at the cost of DevOps complexity.[^22][^23][^24]

**Best for**: cloud-native funds targeting continuous delivery and rapid feature evolution.[^23]

#### Comparative Snapshot

| Path | Architecture | Scalability | Failure Tolerance | Interpretability |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Hierarchical | Master-slave | Moderate | Low | High |
| Distributed | P2P consensus | High | High | Medium |
| RL Multi-Agent | Shared RL env | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Hybrid Rule + AI | Dual layer | Medium | Medium | High |
| Microservices | Event-driven | High | High | Medium |

## Reference Architecture

The diagram below shows how the five attached scanner feeds flow through layered agents—from ingestion to pattern detection, strategy arbitration, risk management and trade execution.

![Multi-Agent Stock Trading System Architecture for Scanner Data Processing](data:image/png;base64,)

Multi-Agent Stock Trading System Architecture for Scanner Data Processing

## Conclusion

The scanner logs prove that **persistent multi-metric winners and losers emerge within minutes of the open**, making them prime targets for both momentum and reversal strategies.  By decomposing the workflow into specialised agents—parsers, pattern recognisers, strategists, risk managers and executors—developers can match **architecture to organisational goals**: tight governance (Path 1), fault-tolerant speed (Path 2), adaptive intelligence (Path 3), explainable AI (Path 4) or cloud-native scale (Path 5).  Whichever path is chosen, enforcing rigorous risk limits and continuous performance feedback keeps the pattern-driven edge durable in the face of regime shifts documented by academic and industry research.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^12][^9][^10][^5][^6][^13][^7][^18]
